architecture.summary: The paper studies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) applied to large language model activations. SAEs are neural networks with an encoder, decoder, and sparsity-inducing loss that aim to decompose dense LLM activations into interpretable latent features. The paper does not propose a new architecture but rather analyzes a failure mode in existing SAE designs: when underlying features form hierarchies, SAEs learn to 'absorb' parent features into child features to increase sparsity, causing seemingly monosemantic latents to fail to activate on cases where they should.
trained_components: Linear regression probes for first-letter classification

biblio.title: A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Sparse Autoencoders fail to robustly decompose hierarchical features; seemingly monosemantic features fail to fire where they should and instead get absorbed into their children features.
Feature absorption is caused by optimizing for sparsity in SAEs whenever the underlying features form a hierarchy.
SAE latents underperform linear regression probes on first-letter identification across Gemma Scope SAEs, with no SAE matching LR probe performance.
Feature absorption rate increases with higher sparsity (L0) and higher SAE width; lower L0 pushes the SAE to absorb dense features like spelling information.
Varying SAE sizes or sparsity is insufficient to solve feature absorption.

The absorption metric cannot capture absorption past layer 17 in Gemma 2 2B because ablation experiments lose causal effect once attention has moved starting letter information from source token to final token position.

claims.dataset: Gemma Scope SAEs (Gemma 2 2B), custom L1 SAEs (Qwen2 0.5B, Llama 3.2 1B), TopK SAEs (Llama 3.2 1B)
claims.statement: Feature absorption is validated empirically on hundreds of LLM SAEs and occurs across all SAE architectures tested (L1 loss SAEs and TopK SAEs).
A formal proof is provided that for hierarchical features (α ⊆ β), feature absorption (α-absorption) decreases SAE sparsity loss while maintaining perfect reconstruction.
